keyword.value: Academic Centre on Peace, Neutrality and Preventive Diplomacy Studies (Proposed)
keyword.showAs: Academic Centre on Peace, Neutrality and Preventive Diplomacy Studies (Proposed)

step.by: Angola, Armenia, Bangladesh, Belarus, Burkina Faso, Burundi, China, Comoros, Congo, Cuba, Djibouti, Egypt, Eswatini, Fiji, Gabon, Ghana, Guinea, Iran (Islamic Republic of), Iraq, Jordan, Kazakhstan, Maldives, Mali, Mongolia, Morocco, Nepal, Nicaragua, Niger, Oman, Pakistan, Russian Federation, Rwanda, Sierra Leone, Singapore, South Sudan, Sudan, Tajikistan, Timor-Leste, Tonga, Türkiye, United Republic of Tanzania, Venezuela (Bolivarian Republic of) and Viet Nam

TLCTerm.showAs: Without reference to a main committee

p.inline: Peacebuilding and sustaining peace: neutrality for peace and security

p.docTitle: Resolution adopted by the General Assembly on 20 May 2026

docTitle.span: The role and importance of a policy of neutrality in maintaining and strengthening international peace, security and sustainable development

container.name: Recalling
container.p: Recalling its relevant resolutions relating to the policy of neutrality, including resolutions 50/80 A of 12 December 1995, 69/285 of 3 June 2015, 71/275 of 2 February 2017, 75/28 of 7 December 2020 and 79/274 of 21 March 2025,

container.name: Guided
container.p: Guided by the purposes and principles of the Charter of the United Nations,

container.name: Commemorating
container.p: Commemorating the eightieth anniversary of the United Nations, and recognizing the urgent need to promote and strengthen multilateralism and that the United Nations plays a central role in this regard,

container.name: Recalling
container.p:
Recalling the Ashgabat outcome documents of 2015
A/70/652-S/2016/20, annex. and 2020,
A/75/651-S/2020/1203, annex. and acknowledging the efforts of the Government of Turkmenistan to institutionalize dialogue on neutrality, preventive diplomacy, and peace, in line with the principles and purposes of the Charter of the United Nations,

container.name: Noting
container.p: Noting the relevant resolutions of the Council of Foreign Ministers of the Organization of Islamic Cooperation that underscore the role of a policy of neutrality in maintaining and strengthening international peace, security and sustainable development,

container.name: Underlining
container.p: Underlining that the national policies of neutrality of some States, without prejudice to the rights and obligations of States under the Charter of the United Nations, can contribute to the strengthening of international peace and security in relevant regions and at the global level and can play an important role in developing peaceful, friendly and mutually beneficial relations between the countries of the world,

container.name: Recognizing
container.p: Recognizing that such national policies of neutrality are aimed at promoting the use of preventive diplomacy, including through the prevention of conflict, mediation, good offices, negotiation, the use of special envoys, informal consultations, peacebuilding and targeted development activities,

container.name: Recognizing also
container.p: Recognizing also the positive role that neutral countries could play in the provision and delivery of humanitarian assistance in situations of complex emergencies and natural disasters, in accordance with the provisions of General Assembly resolution 46/182 of 19 December 1991 on the strengthening of the coordination of humanitarian emergency assistance of the United Nations and the annex thereto, including the humanitarian principles of humanity, neutrality, impartiality and independence, and relevant Assembly resolutions on the subject, including its resolution 79/140 of 9 December 2024, as well as relevant resolutions and agreed conclusions of the Economic and Social Council,

container.name: Underlining
container.p: Underlining that policies of neutrality can contribute to fostering peace and security, and to strengthening subregional, regional and international cooperation and interconnectivity, including in economic, social and humanitarian areas,

container.name: Noting
container.p:
Noting the work of the Group of Friends of Neutrality for Peace, Security and Sustainable Development, an informal group established at the initiative of Turkmenistan in August 2020 and operating on the basis of the final declaration of the first ministerial meeting of the Group
A/77/778, annex. to maintain a wide multilateral dialogue on the practical implementation of the principles of neutrality in preventing conflicts, eliminating the root causes and consequences of conflicts, resolving humanitarian challenges and promoting neutrality in international relations,

container.name: Recognizing
container.p: Recognizing the leadership of Turkmenistan, which, through its policy of permanent neutrality, has promoted dialogue, peace, trust and confidence-building measures in international relations, including through the International Year of Dialogue as a Guarantee of Peace, 2023, and the International Years of Peace and Trust, proclaimed by the General Assembly,

container.name: Underlining
container.p:
Underlining in this regard the holding of the International Forum dedicated to the International Year of Peace and Trust, 2025, the International Day of Neutrality and the thirtieth anniversary of the permanent neutrality of Turkmenistan on 12 December 2025, in Ashgabat, and noting the adoption of its outcome document, the Ashgabat Declaration,
A/80/578-S/2025/833, annex. which emphasizes the importance of the policy of neutrality as an effective tool for strengthening peace, trust and sustainable development,

content.p: Recognizes that neutrality, without prejudice to the rights and obligations of States under the Charter of the United Nations, can embody responsibility rather than indifference, reflecting a principled commitment to conflict prevention through impartiality, good offices, mediation and confidence-building, while maintaining constructive engagement with all parties in the interest of international peace and security;
Invites the Secretary-General to cooperate closely with neutral States, with a view to implementing the principles of preventive diplomacy and using those principles in their mediation activities;

Recommends to all Member States, organizations of the United Nations system and other international, regional and subregional organizations the use, as appropriate, of the full potential of neutral States and mediation in the peaceful settlement of disputes, conflict prevention and resolution in accordance with the Charter of the United Nations;

Once again invites all Member States, organizations of the United Nations system and other international and regional organizations, as well as non‑governmental organizations and individuals, to mark annually the International Day of Neutrality on 12 December by means of education and the holding of events aimed at enhancing the knowledge of the public about the role of a policy of neutrality in maintaining and strengthening international peace and security;

Reiterates the invitation to the Secretary-General to seek the views of Member States and relevant entities of the United Nations system on the role and importance of a policy of neutrality in maintaining and strengthening international peace and security, and to communicate such views to the General Assembly at its eighty-first session for further consideration;

Welcomes the proposal of Turkmenistan, in cooperation with the University of Peace and the United Nations Regional Centre for Preventive Diplomacy for Central Asia, to explore the establishment and hosting, through voluntary contributions, of an Academic Centre on Peace, Neutrality and Preventive Diplomacy Studies, and calls upon Member States and relevant United Nations entities, as appropriate, to consider opportunities for supporting this initiative;

Encourages the effective use of the territories of neutral countries for hosting peace talks and conflict resolution and settlement processes, including through the establishment of dedicated mediation facilities, and notes the proposal of Turkmenistan to set up on its territory the Peace Mediation Chamber, in accordance with the Charter of the United Nations, which would require evaluation by Member States and the United Nations system, as underlined in General Assembly resolution 79/274, without prejudice to existing United Nations mandates;

Reiterates the request to the Secretary-General to develop, within existing resources, under the leadership of all States Members of the United Nations, guidance for upholding and implementing the principles of neutrality for peace and security, with a specific focus on consolidating lessons learned and key principles for the effective application of neutrality in international relations;

Invites the States Members of the United Nations, the Movement of Non‑Aligned Countries and other regional and international organizations to further elaborate the neutrality-based concept of global security, under the auspices of the United Nations, as a basis for consensus-building and strengthening multilateral cooperation, guided by the principles and purposes enshrined in the Charter of the United Nations.
